m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the result of executing the following code to calculate the height of a binary tree?

public int height(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) {
        return 0;
    }
    return 1 + Math.max(height(root.left), height(root.right));
}

Options.OptionText: The code correctly calculates the height of the tree.
The code will enter into an infinite recursion causing a stack overflow.
The code will count the number of nodes in the tree.

m_Item1.Explanation: The code correctly calculates the height of a binary tree by recursively getting the maximum height between the left and right subtrees and adding one for the current level.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following code that checks if a binary tree is a full binary tree:

public boolean isFullBinaryTree(TreeNode node) {
    if (node == null) {
        return true;
    }
    if (node.left == null && node.right == null) {
        return true;
    }
    if (node.left != null && node.right != null) {
        return isFullBinaryTree(node.left) && isFullBinaryTree(node.right);
    }
    return false;
}
What is the result for a completely filled binary tree?

Options.OptionText: The code will throw a NullPointerException.
The code will fail for leaf nodes.

m_Item1.Explanation: For a full binary tree, each node has either 0 or 2 children. The code checks this condition and returns true if the whole tree satisfies it.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the time complexity of the following binary tree traversal function in terms of Big O notation?

public void traverse(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return;
    traverse(root.left);
    System.out.println(root.val);
    traverse(root.right);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The time complexity is O(n) because each node in the binary tree is visited exactly once during the in-order traversal.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the following code for finding the lowest common ancestor (LCA) in a Binary Tree:

public TreeNode findLCA(TreeNode node, TreeNode n1, TreeNode n2) {
    if (node == null) {
        return null;
    }
    if (node == n1 || node == n2) {
        return node;
    }
    TreeNode leftLCA = findLCA(node.left, n1, n2);
    TreeNode rightLCA = findLCA(node.right, n1, n2);
    if (leftLCA != null && rightLCA != null) {
        return node;
    }
    return (leftLCA != null) ? leftLCA : rightLCA;
}
What will happen if nodes n1 and n2 do not exist in the tree?

Options.OptionText: The code will throw a NullPointerException.
The code will return the root of the tree.
The code will enter into an infinite recursion.

m_Item1.Explanation: The function returns null if n1 and n2 do not exist in the tree, ensuring no matching LCA is found.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What would be the result of executing the following code on a skewed binary tree?

public int countNodes(TreeNode node) {
    if (node == null) {
        return 0;
    }
    return 1 + countNodes(node.left) + countNodes(node.right);
}

Options.OptionText: The function will count all the nodes correctly.
The function will throw a stack overflow error.
The function will return the height of the tree.
The function will return the number of leaf nodes only.

m_Item1.Explanation: The function correctly counts each node by performing a recursive traversal of the entire tree, regardless of its shape.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the key characteristic of a perfect binary tree?

Options.OptionText: Every node has either 0 or 2 children.
All levels are completely filled except possibly for the last level, which is filled from left to right.
All internal nodes have two children and all leaves are at the same level.
Nodes have varying numbers of children with unpredictable patterns.

m_Item1.Explanation: In a perfect binary tree, as defined, all internal nodes have exactly two children, and all leaf nodes are at the same level.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
After executing the following mirror function, what will be the state of the binary tree?

public void mirror(TreeNode node) {
    if (node == null) {
        return;
    }
    TreeNode temp = node.left;
    node.left = node.right;
    node.right = temp;
    mirror(node.left);
    mirror(node.right);
}

Options.OptionText: The binary tree will remain unchanged.
All nodes will now point to null.
The binary tree becomes its mirror image.
Each node's left and right children are swapped at the root level only.

m_Item1.Explanation: The code recursively swaps the left and right children of each node, effectively converting the binary tree into its mirror image.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What limitation does this function impose while deleting a node from a binary tree?

public TreeNode deleteNode(TreeNode root, int key) {
    if (root == null) {
        return null;
    }
    if (key < root.val) {
        root.left = deleteNode(root.left, key);
    } else if (key > root.val) {
        root.right = deleteNode(root.right, key);
    } else {
        if (root.left == null) {
            return root.right;
        } else if (root.right == null) {
            return root.left;
        }
        root.val = findMin(root.right).val;
        root.right = deleteNode(root.right, root.val);
    }
    return root;
}
private TreeNode findMin(TreeNode node) {
    while (node.left != null) {
        node = node.left;
    }
    return node;
}

Options.OptionText: It can only delete leaf nodes.
It cannot handle deleting nodes with two children.
It can delete nodes with either one child or no children, but not both.
The function is dependent on the tree being a Binary Search Tree (BST).

m_Item1.Explanation: The algorithm assumes the tree is a Binary Search Tree where keys are ordered with left children smaller and right children larger than the parent node key value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What purpose does the helper function `findMin(TreeNode node)` serve in the `deleteNode` method provided?

Options.OptionText: It finds the maximum value in the tree.
It locates the minimum value node in the right subtree.
It counts the total nodes in the tree.
It replaces the node to be deleted with its immediate predecessor.

m_Item1.Explanation: The `findMin` function is used to find the smallest node in the right subtree, for replacing the node to be deleted when it has two children.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a level order traversal of a binary tree, which data structure is commonly used to implement this traversal efficiently?
m_Item1.Explanation: A Queue is used to achieve level order traversal because it follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO), which is suitable for breadth-first traversal.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the base case for calculating the max depth of a binary tree using recursion?

Options.OptionText: When the node is null, return 0.
When the node is null, return 1.
When the node is a leaf, return 0.
When the node is a leaf, return 1.

m_Item1.Explanation: In the context of recursion for calculating the max depth of a binary tree, the base case is to check if a node is null, in which case return 0 because a null node contributes zero height.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given the Java method signature for calculating max depth: 'public int maxDepth(TreeNode root)', how do you compute the max depth?
m_Item1.Explanation: To compute the maximum depth, you recursively calculate the maximum depth of the left and right subtrees and then add 1 for the current node. Therefore, the correct expression is '1 + Math.max(maxDepth(root.left), maxDepth(root.right))'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the time complexity of finding the max depth of a binary tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: The time complexity of finding the max depth of a binary tree is O(n) because you potentially have to visit every node in the tree exactly once.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Consider the following Java method to compute max depth: 'public static int maxDepth(TreeNode root) { if (root == null) return 0; else return 1 + Math.max(maxDepth(root.left), maxDepth(root.right)); }'. What will be the output for a single-node tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: A single-node tree has a height of 1 because there is only one node in the tree and the height is defined as the number of nodes along the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Java, using a queue for BFS (Breadth-First Search), how do you initialize calculating the max depth of a binary tree?

Options.OptionText: Queue<TreeNode> queue = new LinkedList<>(); if(root != null) queue.offer(root); int depth = 1;
Queue<TreeNode> queue = new LinkedList<>(); if(root != null) queue.add(root); int depth = 0;

m_Item1.Explanation: To perform a level-order traversal using BFS to calculate maximum depth, you can initialize a queue using a LinkedList and add the root node if it's not null. The initial depth should be set to 0 and will be incremented as you traverse each level of the tree.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the correct way to insert a node in a Binary Search Tree (BST) in Java?

Options.OptionText: Use a level-order traversal until a null position is found and insert the node there.
Use a preorder traversal to find the first null position and insert the node there.
Compare the node value with the current node, place in left subtree if smaller, right subtree if larger.
Replace the root node with the new node value.

m_Item1.Explanation: In a BST, the value is compared starting from the root; smaller values go to the left, and larger values go to the right. The process repeats recursively to find the correct position to insert the new node.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given the following code snippet, which part is responsible for inserting recursively in the left subtree?

Options.OptionText: else if (value > node.data) node.right = insert(node.right, value);

m_Item1.Explanation: The line 'if (value < node.data) node.left = insert(node.left, value);' is responsible for recursively calling the insert method on the left subtree when the value to be inserted is smaller than the current node's value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which scenario would you use a queue while inserting a node in a Binary Tree (not a BST)?

Options.OptionText: To ensure breadth-first insertion is maintained.
To traverse the tree in reverse order.
To handle the recursion stack manually.

m_Item1.Explanation: A queue is used in breadth-first insertion to maintain the level order of the tree. Nodes are inserted at the first available spot from left to right.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What exception might you encounter if the insertion code doesn't handle a 'null' root when starting an insertion?
m_Item1.Explanation: Attempting to access or modify an object reference that is not set to an instance will lead to a NullPointerException. It's crucial to check for null and initialize nodes properly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a BST insertion method, what should be the return value at the end of the recursive insert function?

Options.OptionText: The root of the modified subtree.

m_Item1.Explanation: The recursive insert function returns the root of the modified subtree to properly attach the new node to its correct parent, maintaining the tree's structure.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following method in Java to delete a node from a binary tree. What will the method return if the tree is empty?
public TreeNode delete(TreeNode root, int key) {
    if (root == null) {
        // Code block A
    }
    // Other deletion logic
    return root;
}
Choose the correct option for Code block A.
m_Item1.Explanation: When the tree is empty (i.e., root is null), the method should return null, indicating that there's no node to delete.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the time complexity of the delete operation in a balanced binary search tree (BST) when implemented in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a balanced binary search tree, searching, insertion, and deletion operations are performed in O(log n) time complexity due to the structure of the tree.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the following Java code for deleting a leaf node from a binary tree, which line of code is incorrect?
public TreeNode deleteLeaf(TreeNode root, int key) {
    if (root == null) return null;
    if (key < root.val) {
        root.left = deleteLeaf(root.left, key);
    } else if (key > root.val) {
        root.right = deleteLeaf(root.right, key);
    } else {
        if (root.left == null && root.right == null) {
            root = null; // Code block X
        }
    }
    return root;
}
Identify the incorrect line of code.
m_Item1.Explanation: In line 'root = null;', a leaf node is deleted by setting the corresponding reference to null. There is no mistake in logic, but answer C is correct since this is the main action to address deleting a leaf node.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scenario requires replacing a node with its in-order successor during the deletion operation in a binary search tree implemented in Java?

Options.OptionText: When the node to be deleted is a leaf node.
When the node to be deleted has only a left child.
When the node to be deleted has two children.
When the node to be deleted is the root.

m_Item1.Explanation: When a node with two children is being deleted, it is typically replaced with either its in-order predecessor or successor to maintain the properties of the binary search tree.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
In the Java method below for deleting a node in a BST, what condition must be checked before replacing a node with its in-order successor?
public TreeNode deleteNode(TreeNode root, int key) {
    if (root == null) return null;
    if (key < root.val) {
        root.left = deleteNode(root.left, key);
    } else if (key > root.val) {
        root.right = deleteNode(root.right, key);
    } else {
        // Node to be deleted found
        if (root.left == null) {
            return root.right;
        } else if (root.right == null) {
            return root.left;
        }
        TreeNode successor = findMinValueNode(root.right);
        root.val = successor.val;
        root.right = deleteNode(root.right, successor.val);// Code Snippet Y
    }
    return root;
}
What must be true for the code snippet labeled Y?
m_Item1.Explanation: The node to be deleted must have a right child to ensure the existence of an in-order successor for replacement. Root.right is necessary to check as it means the successor exists there for further operations.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function is used to find the number of distinct binary trees that can be formed with n nodes in Java?

Options.OptionText: public int countBinaryTrees(int n) { return factorial(2 * n) / (factorial(n + 1) * factorial(n)); }
public int countBinaryTrees(int n) { return factorial(n) * (factorial(n + 1) / factorial(2 * n)); }
public int countBinaryTrees(int n) { return factorial(n) / (factorial(n + 1) * factorial(n - 1)); }
public int countBinaryTrees(int n) { return factorial(2 * n) / (factorial(n) * factorial(n)); }

m_Item1.Explanation: The number of distinct binary trees with n nodes is given by the nth Catalan number, which is calculated using the formula: C(n) = (2n)! / ((n + 1)! * n!). This matches option A.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the base case condition for enumerating binary trees using dynamic programming in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: The base case for the number of distinct binary trees when there are zero nodes is 1 (empty tree), hence option B is correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which Java method call would correctly calculate the number of unique binary search trees (BST) possible with 3 nodes?
m_Item1.Explanation: The standard nomenclature in algorithms involves 'calculateUniqueBST' for finding unique binary search trees. This method name aligns with the standard semantics.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Java, which of the following expressions evaluates to the number of distinct full binary trees with n internal nodes?
m_Item1.Explanation: The number of distinct full binary trees with n internal nodes is the nth Catalan number, hence the correct option is A.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true about a complete binary tree?

Options.OptionText: Every level, except possibly the last, is completely filled.
All levels must have exactly two children.
It is the same as a full binary tree.
It must be completely filled at every level.

m_Item1.Explanation: A complete binary tree is defined as a binary tree in which every level is completely filled, except possibly the last level, which is filled from the left.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How many leaf nodes are present in a complete binary tree with 'n' nodes?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a complete binary tree with 'n' nodes, the number of leaf nodes is floor(n / 2) because the tree is filled from the top and left to right manner.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a complete binary tree has a height 'h', what is the minimum number of nodes it can contain?
m_Item1.Explanation: For a complete binary tree with height 'h', the minimum number of nodes occurs when all levels except the last are completely filled, and the last level has at least one node, resulting in 2^(h-1) + 1 nodes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following properties is true for an Almost Complete Binary Tree?

Options.OptionText: All levels, except possibly the last, are completely filled.
All leaves are at the same level.
Every parent has exactly two children.
It is a full binary tree.

m_Item1.Explanation: An Almost Complete Binary Tree is completely filled at all levels except possibly the last. In the last level, all nodes are as left as possible.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In an Almost Complete Binary Tree implemented using an array, what is the index of the left child of a node at index i?
m_Item1.Explanation: For a node at index i in an array representation of an Almost Complete Binary Tree, the left child is located at index 2 * i + 1.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true for a Binary Search Tree (BST)?

Options.OptionText: Left child node is always greater than parent node.
Right child node is always smaller than parent node.
Left subtree contains only nodes less than the parent node.
Right subtree can have nodes greater or smaller than the parent node.

m_Item1.Explanation: In a Binary Search Tree (BST), the left subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys less than the node’s key.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the time complexity of searching for an element in a balanced Binary Search Tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a balanced Binary Search Tree, the height of the tree is logarithmic in terms of the number of nodes, thus the time complexity for searching is O(log n).

m_Item1.QuestionText: When inserting a new node into a Binary Search Tree, where does the new node get added?

Options.OptionText: In the middle of the tree

m_Item1.Explanation: When inserting a new node in a Binary Search Tree, it is always added as a leaf node after comparing it with existing nodes starting from the root.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which traversal of a Binary Search Tree results in a sorted list of elements?
m_Item1.Explanation: Inorder traversal of a Binary Search Tree (BST) will give nodes in non-decreasing order, or a sorted list.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a Binary Search Tree, what is the node without a parent called?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a Binary Search Tree, the node without a parent is called the root node.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A Balanced full Binary Tree with 8 leaves has ________________ nodes

m_Item1.QuestionText: The post order traversal of a binary tree is DEBFCA. Find out the pre order traversal
m_Item1.Explanation:
If We Implement Tree with above information then it will be like this
Identify the root: The last element in post-order traversal is 'A'. So, 'A' is the root.
Divide the remaining nodes: The nodes left of 'A' (i.e., DEBFC) are split between the left and right subtrees. In pre-order traversal, the root is visited first, followed by the left subtree, and then the right subtree.
Identify subtrees:
The element before 'A' is 'C', which will be the root of the right subtree.
The element before 'C' is 'F', which will be the right child of 'C'.
The element before 'F' is 'B', which will be the left child of 'C'.
The left part (DEB) before 'B' will be the left subtree of the root 'A'.
Now, we place these nodes into the tree:
​
In Order        :  A B D E C F
Pre Order     :  A B D E C F  
Post Order    :  D E B F C A

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a binary tree, certain null entries are replaced by special pointers which point to nodes higher in the tree for efficiency. These special pointers are called______
m_Item1.Explanation: The idea of threaded binary trees is to make inorder traversal faster and do it without stack and without recursion. A binary tree is made threaded by making all right child pointers that would normally be NULL point to the inorder successor of the node (if it exists). ... The dotted lines represent threads.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A binary tree of depth “d” is an almost complete binary tree if

Options.OptionText: Each leaf in the tree is either at level “d” or at level “d–1”

For any node “n” in the tree with a right descendent at level “d” all the left
descendents of “n” that are leaves, are also at level “d”

m_Item1.Explanation: A binary tree of depth “d” is an almost complete binary tree if each leaf in the tree is either at level “d” or at level “d–1” and for any node “n” in the tree with a right descendent at level “d” all the left descendants of “n” that are leaves, are also at level “d”.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following option is not true about the Binary Search Tree?

Options.OptionText: The left child is always lesser than its parent
The right child is always greater than its parent
The left and right sub-trees should also be binary search trees
In order sequence gives decreasing order of elements

m_Item1.Explanation: In order sequence of binary search trees will always give ascending order of elements. Remaining all are true regarding binary search trees.
